justification: Both domain rules present with testable_assertions. All three architecture decisions present with implementation_notes (PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, RefundService). Acceptance criteria preserves ORDER_STATE_INVALID verbatim. Terminology alignment present and addresses Bird/MJ language differences (order state vs state field, revert-to-pending vs PATCH endpoint). Task context is one specific, actionable paragraph. No invented information detected.

observations.text: Both invariant rules (SHIPPED→PENDING blocked, refund requires DELIVERED) present with concrete testable_assertions
Architecture guidance covers all three decisions: PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, and RefundService with detailed implementation_notes
ORDER_STATE_INVALID error code preserved verbatim in acceptance criteria
Terminology alignment maps Bird's domain language to MJ's technical terms (e.g., 'revert-to-pending' → PATCH with PENDING target state)
Task context is concise, specific, and actionable — tells Shaq exactly what to build and why
Open questions are well-considered and flag genuinely unspecified details without inventing answers
Extra acceptance criteria (refund success case, valid transitions not blocked) add value without inventing new domain rules
Terminology alignment doesn't explicitly call out that Bird says 'order status' while MJ says 'state' — it covers order state but could be more explicit about reconciling the terminology difference
